horus_names.source_note: Von Beckerath 1999, 36–37.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Von Beckerath 1999, 36–37. Because of the uncertainty of reading the sign, the name has also been read as sḫn (sekhen), "The one whom (Horus) has embraced." See Kaplony (1958, 56–57), who rendered the name as "The one who occupies (the palace)." The name Ka is generally accepted today; see Gilroy 2001, 69–70; Jiménez-Serrano 2001, 81 n. 4; Kahl 1994, 38–40; and Köhler and van den Brink 2002, 63.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 1–3, 17–19; von Beckerath 1999, 36–37. Narmer is possibly the King Menes—Egyptian mni (meni), "The established one"—of tradition, although some scholars equate the Horus Aha with Menes. See, lately, the discussion in Raffaele 2003, 106–7.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 33; von Beckerath 1999, 36–37. Although some scholars (e.g., Malek and Forman 1986, 29) deny the existence of this ruler, preferring to interpret the scorpion simply as a ceremonial image, the consensus remains that he was, in fact, king; see T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 56; Dreyer 1992a; H. S. Smith 1992, 244; and Darnell 2002, 14–19.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: A ruler who is attested at the site of Tarkhan, north of the important prehistoric site of Gerza; he may be contemporary with the Horus Ka. For this king, see Dreyer 1992b and Köhler and van den Brink 2002, 70. Raffaele (2003, 114–15) proposes the reading snd (sened), "The Dreadful one."

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: For the name, compare the various royal maceheads from this period as well as the mace held in Narmer's hand in the Smiting Motif scene on the recto of his famous palette. This ruler is attested at Turah and in the eastern Delta.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: The falcon atop the serekh is to be read as part of the name; this ruler is attested at the sites of Turah and Buto.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33
SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33
SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: An otherwise unattested ruler, whose name is found on a jar discovered at Helwan, and who may date to a time after the Horus Ka; see Köhler and van den Brink 2002, 65–67.
horus_names.translation: The one who belongs to (the goddess) Neith

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Ikram 2004. The name may refer to the raised arm of a ruler in the smiting position.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Or perhaps Khenty-pe, "Foremost of the seat [i.e., the throne]." This ruler is sometimes simply referred to as "King B": see T. A. H. Wilkinson 1995 and 2001, p. 53, fig. 2.3, and Ikram 2004, 215.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 3–4, 29–30; von Beckerath 1999, 38–39.

nebty_names.source_note: I have included this entry for the sake of completeness, but it is likely that the hieroglyphs of the vulture and the cobra may not actually be part of Aha's titulary. It has been suggested that the men-hieroglyph refers to the name of the shrine itself, which was called "The Two Ladies are Established"; see Quirke 1990, 23; and T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 203.
nebty_names.translation: The established one

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

golden_horus_names.translation: He who belongs to the Golden One

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 5, 30–31; von Beckerath 1999, 38–39.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 5, 31–32; von Beckerath 1999, 38–39. For the name Wadjet, from a graffito in the Shatt er Rigal where the cobra wꜣḏ-sign is clear, see Legrain 1903, p. 221, fig. 7; and Vergote 1961.

attested_in: Abydos 4, presumably for Ity, as in the Turin Canon

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 6–9; von Beckerath 1999, 38–39. For King Den, see particularly Godron 1990. The king's name has been read a number of different ways, the most common of which are Dewen ("He who spreads [his falcon's] wings]") or Udimu ("He who pours water"). For other variants, see Godron (1990, 11–17), who rightly opts for the reading Den, which he renders as "The slaughterer." For the rendering "severer," see Meltzer 1972; the iconographic motif is well known from the first register on the verso of the Narmer Palette, where the king surveys two rows of decapitated enemies.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Godron (1990, 17–21) renders as "The foreigner," in reference to the king's military incursion into the Sinai; see also T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 206.
throne_names.translation: The highlander

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 9–11; von Beckerath 1999, 40–41. The first sign has also been read ꜥnḏ, giving the name ꜥnḏ-ib (anedj-ib, or Anedjib); see Wb I, 208. Kaplony (1972, col. 63, n. 1) renders as "Brave of heart," lit. "fat-hearted"; for ꜥḏ, see Kahl 2002, 96–100

later_cartouche_names.translation: The eternally beloved of the throne (?)

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: The name is preceded by the expression swty-bity, "He of the sedge and the bee." For the adjective biꜣi, see Kahl 2002, 137–38
throne_names.translation: The one who wished the eternal throne

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 11–14; von Beckerath 1999, 40–41
horus_names.translation: Friend of the (divine) body (i.e., the Ennead)

later_cartouche_names.source_note: For the reading, see Gardiner 1943, 75.
The word smsm must have been meant for smsw.

nebty_names.source_note: T. A. H. Wilkinson (2001, 207) suggests "The guardian of the Two Ladies."
nebty_names.translation: He who is connected to the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 14–15, 32; von Beckerath 1999, 40–41. Cf. the Smiting King motif

nebty_names.translation: (Whose) arm is raised

nebty_names.source_note: That is, have given him the breath of life; cf. the relief from Dahshur where King Snefru is kissed by a leonine goddess (easily accessible in Fakhry 1961, p. 82, fig. 46). This seems more likely than rendering the name as "equal" or "brother" (so T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 204), since an actual identification of the king with a divinity is less likely.
nebty_names.translation: The one whom the Two Ladies have kissed.

nebty_names.source_note: For references, see T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 204–5.
nebty_names.translation: The one who has pacified the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 37; von Beckerath 1999, 42–43.

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Von Beckerath (1999, 42 n. 2) suggests that this name may be a corruption of the previous bḏꜣw. The name has also been read as bꜣw nṯr (bau netjer), "The (very) might of the god."

nebty_names.translation: The Two Ladies are satisfied

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 38; von Beckerath 1999, 42–43. T. Schneider (1996, 258) suggests "Lord and sun god." The name could also be read rꜥ nb(.i) (ra nebi) "Ra is my lord."
horus_names.translation: My lord is the sun god

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: The perfect golden one (?)

golden_horus_names.translation: The golden named one (lit. "name of gold")

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 20, 38–39; von Beckerath 1999, 42–43.
horus_names.translation: The one who belongs to the god

nebty_names.translation: The one who belongs to the divinity of the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

nebty_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 20–21; von Beckerath 1999, 42–43. Horus name remains unknown; see T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 87, and references there. For the weneg-plant and its interpretation, see Wb I, 325:11, and Kahl 2002, 119. For the original reading of the name, see Grdseloff 1944, 291.
nebty_names.translation: The weneg-plant (a designation of the sun god)

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

birth_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 21–22; von Beckerath 1999, 42–43. This Birth name is found on a few Dynasty 4 texts; for the references, see von Beckerath 1999, 42; and T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 88.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 22–24, 40; von Beckerath 1999, 44–45. It is also possible that the Horus Sekhemib and the Seth Peribsen are one and the same king; see T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 90, and the references there.
Less likely, "The dwelling place of Maat"; for the Early Dynastic writing of pr, "house, dwelling," written without the stroke determinative, see Kahl 2002, 149.
horus_names.translation: The resolute one, (for whom) Maat has come forth

nebty_names.translation: The resolute one of the Two Ladies, (for whom) Maat has come forth

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

nebty_names.translation: (For whom) Their will has come forth

seth_names.source_note: Von Beckerath 1999, 44–45. Less likely, "The dwelling place of their will."
seth_names.translation: Seth, (for whom ?) their will has come forth

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: (For whom) Their will has come forth

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 34–36, 47–48; von Beckerath 1999, 44–45. That is, Horus.
Horus/Seth 2 form: the king reconciled the Seth and Horus traditions; the serekh is topped by BOTH Horus and Seth animals. See the accompanying Two Ladies entries which repeat this dual form.
horus_names.translation: The two powerful ones have appeared, the two lords being satisfied with him

attested_in: Abydos 14; according to Kitchen (1993, 154), this refers to King Khasekhemwy

nebty_names.source_note: Horus/Seth 2 form: the king reconciled the Seth and Horus traditions; the serekh is topped by BOTH Horus and Seth animals. See the accompanying Two Ladies entries which repeat this dual form. If the two signs nbwy in the last phrase were placed in honorific transposition, this part of the name might read ḥtp nbwy im.f, "The two lords within him are satisfied."
nebty_names.translation: The two powerful ones have appeared, the two lords being satisfied with him

nebty_names.source_note: Horus/Seth 2 form: the king reconciled the Seth and Horus traditions; the serekh is topped by BOTH Horus and Seth animals. See the accompanying Two Ladies entries which repeat this dual form
nebty_names.translation: The two powerful ones have appeared, the golden one of their bodies

seth_names.source_note: Horus/Seth 2 form: the king reconciled the Seth and Horus traditions; the serekh is topped by BOTH Horus and Seth animals. See the accompanying Two Ladies entries which repeat this dual form.
seth_names.translation: The two powerful ones have appeared, the two lords being satisfied with him

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 26; von Beckerath 1999, 44–45. For this king, see lately Ryholt 2008.
horus_names.translation: The one whom a ka has made perfect

later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Sokar

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Note that this king may not actually have existed; Goedicke (1956) has proposed that the entry in the Royal Canon of Turin simply contains the word ḏfꜣ, "missing, lacking," indicating a gap in the scribe's sources, thus effectively rendering a "King Lacuna." Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk. Leprohon marks this king with a `(?)` uncertainty hedge.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Lit. "divine of body." Perhaps less likely, the name may mean "The divine one of the (divine) Corporation," with the last term in the name referring to the Ennead, for which see Wb III, 357:18 and Hannig 2006b, 1972.

later_cartouche_names.source_note: The name Djoser, by which this king is better known, appears only in later records. It is attested as early as the Middle Kingdom in Pap. Westcar (1,14) and on a seated figure of Djoser dedicated by King Senwosret II in Karnak Temple (statue Berlin 7702, for which see Roeder 1913, 144; Wildung 2003, 75–76; and Baud 2007, 78–79). It is also known from the Ramesside Lists: Saqqara List (no. 12) offers the short moniker Djoser, while the Abydos List (no. 16) gives [///] ḏsr-sꜣ (/// djeser sa), "[///] Sacred of protection," and the Turin Canon (3,5) offers ḏsr it (?) (djeser it), "The sacred one and sovereign (?)." By the Ptolemaic period, the king was certainly known by the two names Netjerikhet and Djoser, as shown on the famous Famine Stela, for which see Barguet 1953; and Ritner 2003, 387.

nebty_names.translation: The one (whose) body is divine

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: Lit. "powerful of body." Less likely, "The powerful one of the (divine) Corporation."

nebty_names.source_note: For an easily accessible drawing of the name, see T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, p. 98, fig. 3.5; see also Kahl 1995, 134–35.
nebty_names.translation: The sacred one of the Two Ladies, (long) may you live

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

golden_horus_names.source_note: This is the first attested use of the hieroglyphic group of the falcon on a standard over the "gold" sign; see Dobrev 1993, 193 n. 47; von Beckerath 1999, 18; and Baud 2007, 171.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

horus_names.source_note: The identification of the Horus Sanakht with King Nebka is ascertained by a fragmentary sealing from Beit Khallaf, north of Abydos; see T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 101–3; Baud 2007, 19–20, 41; and Ryholt 2008, 170. Both the Turin Canon (3,4) and the Abydos King List (no. 15) also give a cartouche name Nebka and place the king at the beginning of Dynasty 3, before Djoser; the presence of a royal name within a cartouche, however, should place him toward the end of the dynasty, as T. A. H. Wilkinson (2001, 101) points out.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Or perhaps simply "Possessor of a ka."
throne_names.translation: Lord of the ka

horus_names.source_note: Lit. "High of the White Crown." For this Horus name, found on a stela dated stylistically to a time close to the end of Dynasty 3, see Vandier 1968; and Kahl 1995, 164–65. See also T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 104–5; and Baud 2007, 40–42. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.

later_cartouche_names.source_note: The form Hwni, by which this king is usually known, is simply a later variant of the contemporary name nsw Hwi; cf. Wb III, 49:5–7. See Meltzer 1971; von Beckerath, 1999, 48 n. 8; and Baud 2007, 25–29.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Huni was the first king to enclose his Throne name in a cartouche; see Dobrev 1993, 193 n. 49.
throne_names.translation: The smiting king

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 57. The entry should perhaps read sḏ sy, (sedj sy), "It is broken," for which see Ryholt 2008, 164. This royal name is not attested anywhere else, and, like the following entry, may simply be an indication of a gap in the Ramesside scribe's records. Perhaps the word sḏs is meant for sḏb, "broken," for which see Wb IV, 381–82, and Hannig 2003, 1274–75. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Like King "Hudjefa" of Dynasty 2, this ruler may not actually have existed, the name simply designating a gap in the Ramesside scribe's records. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: That is, "The perfect one belonging to the ka of Re." The name is attested only in the Abydos List (no. 19) but not in contemporary records. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Like the preceding entry, this ruler is attested only in a later list, where he appears before Huni. A contemporary King Nebka(?) may in fact have existed; see Černý 1958; Dodson 1981; Kahl 1995, 202–3; T. A. H. Wilkinson 2001, 101–3; and Ryholt 2008, 170 and n. 34. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The possessor of the ka of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

golden_horus_names.source_note: From this time on, the Golden Horus became a regular part of the royal titulary; see Dobrev 1993, 189.

horus_names.translation: The lord of Maat has made me perfect

nebty_names.translation: Possessor of Maat

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: If the term is not an abbreviated form of a theophoric name—as is the name of Snefru's son, Khufu—one could also simply render snfrw, "The one who has been made perfect," reading the form as an imperfective passive participle, for which see Edel 1955, § 638 f.
throne_names.translation: <God N> has made me perfect

golden_horus_names.source_note: Perhaps referring to Khufu and his father Snefru, for which see Dobrev 1993, 189–94.

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 72–83; von Beckerath 1999, 52–53. Reading as another passive participle, from "to follow, adhere to" (Wb II, 192: 3–5). Gardiner and Peet (1955, 58) and T. Schneider (1996, 149) prefer the meaning "to strike" for the verb mḏd (Wb II, 191: 14–17). I have opted for the former rendering because of the king's Two Ladies name. For the problem of interpreting the name, see Dobrev 1993, 188 n. 33; and Baud 1998, 18 n. 5.

nebty_names.translation: Who has adhered to the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Throne, short form.
throne_names.translation: He protects me

throne_names.source_note: Throne, full form. This fuller name is the theophoric version of the abbreviated "personal"—and commonly used today— name of Khufu; see Dobrev 1993, 195 n. 55.
throne_names.translation: Khnum, he protects me

nebty_names.translation: Who has manifested (himself) by means of the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: I have opted for the reading Radjedef because of its later Greek form Rathotis (Waddell 1940, 102). A reading "Djedefre" would give us "Re endures," lit. "He endures, (namely) Re," but such a phrase would not refer to the king himself, which a name ought to do. It is also to be noted that Radjedef was the first to use the designation sꜣ rꜥ, "the son of Re," to introduce his Throne name.
throne_names.translation: Re is his stability

nebty_names.translation: Who is strong by means of the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: If the name were to be patterned after that of his half-brother Radjedef, we should then read Ra-khaf, "Re is his (very) appearance," which would make very good sense, but I have opted for the customary "Khafre" because of the later Greek rendering Chephren.
throne_names.translation: He appears (as) Re

later_cartouche_names.source_note: This royal prince's name is surrounded by a cartouche in a Middle Kingdom graffito found in the Wadi Hammamat, which lists Kings Khufu, Radjedef, and Khafre, followed by the two names Hordjedef and Baufre, also written inside cartouches (see Drioton 1954); the name is also known from Pap. Westcar 4,17–18. It is not likely that Prince Baufre actually reigned as king; see Redford 1986, 25; Vercoutter 1992, 285–86; T. Schneider 1996, 134; and Baud 1999, 548, 631. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: His bas are those of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

nebty_names.source_note: For this interpretation of the name, which can be compared to other Two Ladies names from the same dynasty, see Dobrev 1993, 188.
nebty_names.translation: The bull <by means of> the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: The established one of the kas of Re

golden_horus_names.source_note: Leprohon prints Golden Horus: (unknown). See Dobrev 1993, 189 n. 37.

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 101–3; von Beckerath 1999, 54–55. An additional king named Thamphthis, for whom no Egyptian sources are known, is sometimes added at the end of Dynasty 4; see von Beckerath 1999, 54 n. 5.
horus_names.translation: The noble one of the (divine) Corporation

nebty_names.translation: The noble one <by means of> the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: His ka is noble

horus_names.translation: The one who has accomplished Maat

nebty_names.translation: The one who has accomplished Maat

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

birth_names.source_note: For this meaning of sꜣḥ, see Wb IV, 21:15–20; and Hannig 2006b, 2088.

golden_horus_names.source_note: If this interpretation of the name is correct, Sahure seems to be repeating Khufu's association of himself with his predecessor in his Golden Horus name; cf. Dobrev 1993, 190 n. 41.

horus_names.source_note: Less likely, "Possessor of crowns."

nebty_names.translation: Possessor of appearances

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

birth_names.source_note: Leprohon labels this entry "Birth (?) name" — uncertainty hedge on whether this was a Birth name proper.

golden_horus_names.source_note: Or perhaps literally "The three powers," referring to the first three kings of the dynasty, with the three sekhem- scepter hieroglyphs replacing the falcon sign in the name; see Dobrev 1993, 190 n. 41.

nebty_names.translation: Who has appeared by means of the Two Ladies
Strong of appearances <by means of> the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: The perfect one is the one whom the ka of Re has engendered (lit. "made")

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: The noble one of the ka of Re

birth_names.source_note: The name may simply be another diminutive; see Scheele-Schweitzer 2007.

nebty_names.translation: Who is perfect by means of the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: The (very) perfection of Re

throne_names.source_note: The name nfr.f ra is a contemporary reinterpretation of the original nfr ra; see Verner 1985. The reading Renefer/Reneferef, with a meaning of "Re is perfect," which has also been proposed by Verner (1985), focuses on the sun god rather than on the king himself; hence my preference for the reading Nefer(ef)re.
throne_names.translation: He is perfect (in the manner of) Re

birth_names.source_note: Leprohon labels this entry "Birth (?)" — uncertainty hedge. One might suggest "The delayed one" (CDME, 23), perhaps referring to a baby whose birth was overdue. Alternatively, perhaps the name means something like "The (one with the bushy?) eyebrows" (CDME, 23). Perhaps simpler, the name may be a nickname of Niuserre; see Scheele-Schweitzer 2007.

horus_names.source_note: Lit. "the place of the heart."
horus_names.translation: The favorite of the Two Lands

nebty_names.translation: The favorite of the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: Who belongs to the power of Re

birth_names.source_note: Leprohon labels this entry "Birth (?) 1" — uncertainty hedge.

Leprohon labels this entry "Birth (?) 2" — uncertainty hedge. The name Ikau was simply an abbreviation of the fuller name Ikauhor (Gauthier 1907, 123; von Beckerath 1999, 58); the latter was itself derived from the Throne name Menkauhor (Edel 1960, 79–80; and Fischer 1989a, 23 n. 6), which may again mean that the name was not actually the king's Birth name.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: The established one of the kas of Horus

birth_names.source_note: The name, which could simply be a diminutive, may be derived from the imperative verb iz, "go!" (Wb I, 126:8–16), an expression possibly uttered by a midwife at the time of the birth.

nebty_names.translation: Enduring of appearances <by means of?> the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: The enduring one of the ka of Re
The enduring one of the ka of Horus

birth_names.source_note: If, as suggested by Scheele-Schweitzer (2007, 93–94), the name is read wn is, that is, the verb followed by a strengthening particle, the name might perhaps mean something like "The one who truly exists," lit. "who exists indeed."

horus_names.source_note: Unas's titulary is discussed by Aufrère (1982, 52–53), who has suggested an unattested Throne name *Wadjkare.
horus_names.translation: The sturdy one of the Two Lands

nebty_names.translation: The one who is sturdy by means of the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

birth_names.source_note: Some examples of the name show the epithet sꜣ rꜥ, "the son of Re," written within the cartouche and preceding the name Teti.

horus_names.translation: The one who has satisfied the Two Lands

nebty_names.translation: The one who has satisfied the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Leprohon prints Throne: (unknown). See Aufrère (1982, 53–54), who, on the analogy of Pepy I, has proposed an unattested Throne name of *Sehetepre for Teti.
throne_names.translation: (unknown)

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 145; von Beckerath 1999, 62–63. On this obscure king, who probably reigned for a short period of time between Teti and Pepy I, see Baud and Dobrev 1995, 59–63, and Baud 1999, 625, and the references given there. His memory lived on, as he is also attested in the Ramesside Abydos List (no. 35) between the two aforementioned rulers. His identification with the King Iti mentioned in two graffiti in the Wadi Hammamat (Couyat and Montet 1913, nos. 168, 169) is highly speculative; see Baud and Dobrev 1995, 60 and n. 95.

nebty_names.translation: (unknown)

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Leprohon labels this entry "Throne/Birth (?)" — uncertainty about whether the form is a Throne or Birth name.
throne_names.translation: The strong one belonging to the ka of Re

birth_names.source_note: As with King Teti, some instances show the epithet sꜣ rꜥ, "the son of Re," written within the cartouche. For the transliteration of the name, see Fischer 1989b.

golden_horus_names.source_note: Perhaps referring to himself and his two predecessors; see Dobrev 1993, 190 n. 41. Compare the Golden Horus names of Khufu and Sahure.

nebty_names.translation: Beloved of the Two Ladies' bodies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: With the mention of Horus here, the king is presumably referring to himself. This earlier Throne name of Pepy I is known from graffiti at Hatnub and at Tomas in Nubia (for the latter text, see Strudwick 2005, 150). Dobrev (1993, 196 n. 57) has noted that this is a rare example of a nonsolar theophoric Throne name.
throne_names.translation: Perfect is the protection of Horus
Beloved of Re

nebty_names.translation: (The one for whom) The appearances of the Two Ladies are alive

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: The one whom Re has loved

birth_names.source_note: Leprohon labels this entry "Birth 1". As with Kings Teti and Pepy I, the epithet sꜣ rꜥ can sometimes precede the name within the cartouche.

nebty_names.translation: The divine one of the appearances of the Two Ladies

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Re

horus_names.source_note: Gauthier 1907, 176; von Beckerath 1999, 64–65.

nebty_names.translation: unknown (?)

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Von Beckerath (1999, p. 64, n. 3) has suggested reading the sign in the Abydos List (no. 39) as ḏfꜣ, "lacuna," but this presumably indicates a missing divine name nmty.
throne_names.translation: The one whom Re has loved, [Nemty] is his protection

birth_names.source_note: As the asterisk indicates, this queen is not actually attested in contemporary documents; for a survey of the reign, see Coche-Zivie 1972, 119–32; and the remarks in Baud 1999, 631. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The divine one of the ka of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The established one of the ka of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

birth_names.source_note: The word "lord" presumably refers to a divine master.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Abydos 43 gives both names Neferkare and Nebi in the single cartouche; Gauthier 1907, 182–84; von Beckerath 1999, 66–67. The king's name is attested contemporaneously from that of his pyramid, ḏd-ꜥnḫ-nfr-kꜣ-rꜥ (djed-ankh-nefer-ka-re), "Neferkare is stable and alive," from a fragmentary stela found in one of the rooms belonging to Queen Iput at Saqqara (Jéquier 1933, 53).
throne_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Re.

later_cartouche_names.source_note: As with the previous ruler, both names Djedkare and Shemay are given in a single cartouche in Abydos 44; Gauthier 1907, 184; von Beckerath 1999, 66–67. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The enduring one of the ka of Re

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Leprohon labels this as "Second later cartouche name". Perhaps also "the nomad," for which see T. Schneider 1998, 25, and the references there.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Abydos 45 gives both names in the single cartouche; Gauthier 1907, 185; von Beckerath 1999, 66–67. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Re

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Leprohon labels this as "Second later cartouche name".

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: For the reading of the sign as the Min emblem (Gardiner Sign List R 22), as opposed to the door bolt sign (Gardiner Sign List S 29), see von Beckerath 1999, 66 n. 5. The divine emblem sign was subsequently misread by the Ramesside scribe (Abydos 47 [Gauthier 1907, 186]) as the door bolt, which gives the name Seneferka, "The one who makes the ka of Re perfect" (e.g., Gardiner 1961, 437). The designation "Later cartouche name" for this king comes from von Beckerath's suggestion (1999, 66 n. 5) that the gold plaque that contains the name, along with the next king's (cf. Gardiner 1961, 437 n. 4), is not contemporary. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of (the god) Min

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: Who belongs to the ka of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33
SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: The name is found on a scarab, for which see Petrie 1917, p. x and pl. 10, no.7.10. See also Abydos 49, which gives both names in the single cartouche.
throne_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Re

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Horus

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Abydos 51; Turin (4,8) only writes the name as nfr-ḥꜣt but adds the epithet šri, "The younger," to the name; Gauthier 1907, 189; von Beckerath 1999, 68–69. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Re

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Leprohon labels this as "Second later cartouche name".

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

later_cartouche_names.source_note: As with the previous king named Neferkamin (8:8), the Ramesside scribe (Abydos 52) read the name as Seneferka; the other Ramesside scribe (Turin 4,9) simply offers the name "Nefer." See Gauthier 1907, 190; von Beckerath 1999, 68–69. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one of the ka of Min

later_cartouche_names.source_note: Leprohon labels this as "Second later cartouche name". Here one is tempted to render the full name as Neferkamin the Fair.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33
SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Turin (4,10) only gives the Birth name Ibi. This king is known from an unfinished pyramid at South Saqqara, for which see Jéquier 1935; and von Beckerath 1962, 141, 144. Abydos 53, which gives only the Throne name, offers the plural kꜣw (kau), ". . . the kas of Re." Gauthier 1907, 190; von Beckerath 1999, 68–69.
throne_names.translation: The exalted (lit. "high") one of the ka of Re

horus_names.source_note: The name is known from Coptos Decree (h), for which see Hayes 1946, 5, 11–13, 20.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Abydos 54, which gives only the Throne name; Gauthier 1907, 190; von Beckerath 1999, 68–69.
throne_names.translation: The perfect one of the kas of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Abydos 55, which gives only the Throne name; Gauthier 1907, 190; von Beckerath 1999, 68–69.
throne_names.translation: The perfect one of the kas of Horus

later_cartouche_names.source_note: This ruler is sometimes identified with the Horus Demedj-ib-tawy; see W. S. Smith 1971, 995; and, tentatively, Kitchen 1993, 155. Ramesside-attested only — no contemporary attestation per Leprohon's headword asterisk.
later_cartouche_names.translation: The perfect one is the one whom the ka of Re has engendered

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33
SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Attested on a fragmentary piece of papyrus from Elephantine (Pap. Berlin 10523), for which see Möller 1911, pl. 5, 6/9 and X/XV. The first sign, which is probably the sekhem-scepter, could also be read as the ankh-sign, which would give the name ankh ka re, "The living one is the (very) ka of Re."
throne_names.translation: The powerful one of the ka of Re

horus_names.translation: Who has united (lit. "assembled") the will (lit. "mind") of the Two Lands

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Attested in Coptos Decree (r), for which see Hayes 1946, 6, 20.
throne_names.translation: The flourishing one of the ka of Re

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Known from two graffiti left in the Wadi Hammamat (nos. 168 and 169, for which see Couyat and Montet 1913, 94); the latter text gives the name of the king's pyramid as mr bꜣw-iti (mer bau iti), "The pyramid of the ba-power of Iti."
throne_names.translation: Iti

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: Known from a graffito left in the Wadi Hammamat (no. 206, for which see Couyat and Montet 1913, 103), which details an expedition undertaken on behalf of an "Imhotep," whose name is written in a cartouche; see also Gauthier 1907, 143.
throne_names.translation: The one who has come in peace

birth_names.translation: The one who is at peace

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

throne_names.source_note: This name may simply be a short form of the previous one; see von Beckerath 1999, p. 70, n. 3.
throne_names.translation: /// of Re ///

birth_names.source_note: The name, written in a cartouche, is found on a fragmentary limestone relief from Middle Egypt near Dara, a site on the west bank of the Nile between Meir and Asyut, for which see Kamal 1912, 132–33. A pyramid found nearby may well belong to this king (cf. Fakhry 1961, 202–4) but the attribution cannot be proven; see Grajetzki 2006, 7–8. The name may be a shortened form of a theophoric name, "<God X> is the one who protects me."

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

birth_names.source_note: From a graffito found in the Gebel Silsileh area, written by the King's Son and Chief Lector-priest Isu-ankhu ("Isu is alive"), where the element "Isu" of the compound name is written in a cartouche; see Weigall 1908, 110.

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33

birth_names.source_note: The royal name comes from the basiliphoric name of a woman called Zat-Iytjenu ("The daughter of <King> Iytjenu") on an Eighth Dynasty stela found at Saqqara; like the previous entry, the element "Iytjenu" of the compound name is written in a cartouche; see Fischer 1963, 36, and pl. 6.
birth_names.translation: The one who has come (already) distinguished

source_citation.edition: SBL Writings from the Ancient World 33
